facts_disclosed: Left shared studio, working from home now

turns.content: So I made a pretty big change recently and I'm still adjusting. Gave up my studio space and I'm working from home now.

That is a big change — was it something you'd been thinking about for a while, or did it come together more suddenly? Going from a dedicated studio to working from home is a significant shift in how your workday feels.

It was more of a financial decision honestly. I lost a major client a couple months ago and had to take a hard look at my expenses. The studio rent was one of the biggest line items and I just couldn't justify it anymore, especially with the income dip.

That makes sense — when income drops unexpectedly, fixed costs like studio rent become hard to sustain. It sounds like a pragmatic decision even if it wasn't necessarily what you wanted. Losing a major client and then having to restructure your overhead on top of that is a lot to deal with at once.

Where were you working before — a shared space, or your own private studio?

It was a shared studio with a couple other designers. Which was nice because even though I mostly work at night when they weren't there, just knowing other creative people were around during the day was grounding somehow. Like I'd come in sometimes in the afternoon and there'd be someone to bounce ideas off of or just complain about clients with.

That kind of ambient creative community is hard to replicate — it's not like you're collaborating on projects necessarily, but having other designers around creates this informal support network. Someone to show a rough draft to, someone who gets why you're stressed about a kerning decision. The social texture of a shared workspace is one of those things you don't fully appreciate until it's gone.

How's the home setup working so far? Have you been able to carve out a decent workspace?

i set up a corner of my apartment with my desk and monitors. spent way too long getting the lighting right — like a full day testing different bulb temperatures because i need accurate color rendering for design work. it's functional. it's fine. honestly it's a little depressing but i'm trying not to think about it that way

The lighting thing is legitimately important for design work, not procrastination — bad color temperature will throw off every palette you build, so that's time well spent. And getting the physical setup right makes the space feel more intentional, even if it's a corner of your apartment rather than a proper studio.

The "it's functional, it's fine, it's a little depressing" is really honest. There's a grief to downsizing your creative space, even when it's the right financial move. The apartment corner doesn't signal "I'm a professional designer" the same way a shared studio does — both to yourself and to the world.

Are you finding the work itself is affected, or is it more the emotional and social side that's hard?

the work is honestly fine? like the actual designing part doesn't care where i am, a screen is a screen. and there are some upsides — no commute means i can roll out of bed at midnight and start working in my pajamas which, let's be real, is basically what i was doing anyway but now without the 20-minute bike ride at 1am. it's the isolation that gets me

The commute elimination is real — especially for a night-owl workflow, cutting out a late-night bike ride in both directions gives you almost an hour back. And being honest about the pajamas thing is fair; the output doesn't care about dress code.

But isolation for a creative person is a genuine concern, not just a comfort issue. Ideas need friction with other people. Even overhearing another designer's phone call with a client or seeing what someone else is working on can spark something. When you're solo at home, your only inputs are what you deliberately seek out, and that narrows your creative world over time.

Are you staying in touch with the other designers from the shared space, or was it more of a proximity friendship?

we have a group chat and we get coffee sometimes but it's not the same. like having someone three meters away who you can just turn to and say 'does this typeface look weird to you' is completely different from sending a screenshot over LINE and waiting for a reply. the feedback loop is slower and more formal

That distinction is really precise — the immediacy and informality of in-person creative feedback is almost impossible to replicate digitally. A quick "does this look weird" over your shoulder takes five seconds and you get an honest gut reaction. A screenshot over messaging becomes a considered response, and by the time you get it you've already second-guessed yourself three times.

A few things that might help with the isolation without the cost of a studio:

- **Coworking day passes** — some spaces let you drop in occasionally without a monthly commitment. Even one day a week around other people can reset your social meter.
- **Online design communities with voice/video** — Discord servers or regular video calls with designer friends where you can share screens and get real-time feedback.
- **Coffee shop sessions** — not for serious color-critical work, but for sketching, research, or admin tasks. Just being around ambient human activity helps.

Does any of that feel feasible, or does the budget situation rule out even occasional coworking?

the coffee shop thing i've actually been doing a bit. there's a good one near my apartment that's open late and i'll go there for the ideation phase of projects, just sketching and reading. can't do actual production work because the screen situation is obviously not ideal but it helps with the cabin fever. coworking day passes are a good idea, i hadn't thought about that

That's a smart split — using the coffee shop for the creative thinking phases where you don't need color accuracy or dual monitors, and saving the home setup for production. You're basically creating two workspaces out of necessity, each optimized for a different part of your process.

For the coworking thing, look for spaces that cater to creative freelancers specifically rather than generic hot-desking offices. Some cities have design-focused coworking spaces where you'd naturally end up around other designers and illustrators. Even once or twice a month could give you that social creative energy you're missing.

How are things looking financially with the savings from dropping the studio? Is it making a noticeable difference?

yeah the savings are significant honestly. the rent on the studio was not trivial, and splitting it three ways was already the affordable option. now that money goes to... well, surviving while i build up the client roster again. it's the right decision. i just wish it didn't feel like going backwards, you know? like i worked hard to get to the point where i had a real studio and now i'm back at my apartment desk like i'm 23 again

That feeling of regression is really understandable — there's a narrative in creative careers where you're supposed to keep leveling up, and going from studio to apartment can feel like moving backward on that progression. But the context matters: you made a smart financial decision during a tough period. That's not going backward, that's adapting.

And honestly, the quality of your work doesn't change based on the prestige of where you made it. Clients aren't visiting your studio — they're seeing the deliverables. Some of the best design work in history was made in apartments and bedrooms and kitchen tables.

When the finances stabilize and the client roster fills back out, you can always get a studio space again if you want one. This doesn't have to be permanent. It's a chapter, not the whole story.

yeah. yeah you're right. honestly hearing it framed as adapting instead of failing is helpful. i've been kind of beating myself up about it. okay i'm going to go do some work in my perfectly-lit apartment corner and try to appreciate the lack of a 1am commute. thanks for this

Go make something great from your well-calibrated apartment corner. The lighting setup alone shows you're taking the space seriously, even if it's smaller than what you had before. And seriously — look into those coworking day passes when you're ready. A little bit of creative community goes a long way. You've got this.
